text: 1 | P a g e
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
REPUBLIC OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal made under
Section 331(1) of the Code of Criminal
Procedure Act No.15 of 1979 read with
Article 138 of the Constitution of the
Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri
Lanka.
Court of Appeal No:
CA/HCC/0127/2023 Sembukutty Arachchilage Nishshanka
High Court of Anuradhapura
Case No: HC/02/2016
Accused-Appellant
Vs.
The Hon. Attorney General
Attorney General's Department
Colombo-12
Complainant-Respondent
BEFORE : P. Kumararatnam, J.
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
COUNSEL : Shavindra Fernando, PC, with Nipun
Samaratunga, Sandani Wijendra and
Amanda Imbulana for the Appellant.
Sudharshana De Silva, PC, ASG with Malik
Azeez, SC for the Respondent.
2 | P a g e
ARGUED ON : 27/01/2026 DECIDED ON : 26/03/2026
JUDGMENT
P. Kumararatnam, J.
The above-named Accused-Appellant (hereinafter referred to as the
Appellant) being aggrieved by the conviction and the sentence imposed on
him on 23/03/2023 by the Learned High Court Judge of Colombo, preferred
this appeal to this Court well within the appealable time.
The Appellant was indicted by the Attorney General in the High Court of
Anuradhapura with one charge of possession of an automatic T-56 gun
punishable under Section 22(3) of the Firearms Act as amended by Act No.
22 of 1996 and the 2nd charge for the possession of 26 live bullets punishable
under Section 27(1) of the Explosive Act.
After trial, the Appellant was found guilty on both counts and the Learned
High Court Judge of Anuradhapura has imposed a sentence of life
imprisonment for the 1st count and fine of Rs.10,000/- with a default
sentence of 03 months simple imprisonment imposed on the 2nd count.
3 | P a g e
The Learned President’s Counsel for the Appellant informed this court that
the Appellant has given consent to argue this matter in his absence. During
the argument he was connected via the Zoom platform from prison.
The Learned President’s Counsel, on behalf of the Appellant had raised
the following appeal grounds:
1. The evidence led by the prosecution does not support the prosecution
version.
2. The learned High Court Judge has shifted the burden of proof to the
defence.
At the trial, PW1 IP/Kumarasinghe, and PW2 SI/Ubayananda, were called
by the prosecution to give evidence on behalf of the prosecution. The
Government Analyst Report was admitted and marked under Section 420 of
the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. Further, the prosecution had marked
productions P1-P5. The Appellant had made a dock statement and closed his
case.
Background of the case
According to PW1, he was the Officer-In-Charge of the Nochchiyagama Police
Station at the time of the raid. PW1 had received some information from a
private informant that a person who is a permanent resident of his police
division had smuggled a weapon from Colombo. This witness had not entered
this information in the relevant books but said that he communicated the
same to the Assistant Superintendent of the police division and had left for
the raid. After mobilising his team around the place called Galdiwewa,
Pahamunegoma and Ralapanawa, he had directly gone to a house said to be
belonging to the Appellant and had then arrested him and recovered an
assembled T-56-gun with a magazine under a mattress in the room.
4 | P a g e
PW2, was called to give evidence to corroborate the evidence given by PW1.
The gun and the magazine which were recovered during the raid contained
26 live bullets and was handed over to the reserve Police Constable 48492
Jayakody marked under Production Register No.99/11.
The productions alleged to have been recovered from the Appellant had been
forwarded to the Government Analyst. According to the Government
Analyst’s Report, the gun had been categorised as an automatic weapon and
the bullets were categorised as explosives.
In respect of the 1st ground of appeal advanced by the Appellant, the learned
President’s Counsel contended that the so-called raid conducted by the
police is a fabricated and premeditated one.
Under this ground the learned Presidents Counsel contended that the notes
taken down by PW1, which was highly relied on by the prosecution, does not
contain relevant material.
In cases such as this, the police would essentially act as the main source of
information, and such information would later become the evidence that is
used in a criminal prosecution. Accordingly, the police would bear the
primary responsibility to acquire such evidence as accurately as possible
without any contradictions, being the investigative arm of the state.
Thereby, it is of utmost importance that the best evidence rule is followed in
criminal trials, where an accused would be considered innocent until proven
guilty where the guilt of such an accused would have to be proven beyond
reasonable doubt and not just on a mere balance of probabilities.
The concept of “reasonable doubt” refers to the legal principle which
establishes that insufficient evidence prevents the conviction of a defendant
of a crime. The prosecution would bear the weight of proving the defendant’s
guilt in respect of a crime with which he has been charged to the judge, in
order to prove why the defendant should be convicted. The phrase “beyond
5 | P a g e
a reasonable doubt” would therefore mean that the evidence and arguments
brought forward by the prosecution to establish the defendant’s guilt is done
so clearly, in a manner that it is accepted as fact by any rational person.
As such, the prosecution would have an obligation to collect and present the
best possible evidence to prove the accused’s guilt; where the investigating
officers are duty bound to collect the best possible evidence, and the public
prosecutor is bound to present the best possible evidence to prove an alleged
fact or circumstance leading to the commission of the offence. Failing to lead
the best evidence could make the prosecution’s case vulnerable, as an
adverse inference could be drawn against such evidence on the basis that
such a failure led to a suppression of the best evidence.
Bradford Smith, Law Commission, WWW.smithlitigation.com 2014 states
that:
“Good police note taking is important for two reasons. First, it invariably
bolsters the credibility of the police officer giving evidence. Second, it
promotes the proper administration of criminal justice by facilitating the
proof of facts”.
In the case of Manu Sharma [Manu Sharma v. State (NCT of Delhi), (2010)
6 SCC 1 it was held:
“What is the significance of requiring an investigating officer/officer in
charge of a police station to maintain a diary? The purpose and the object
seems to be quite clear that there should be fairness in investigation,
transparency and a record should be maintained to ensure a proper
investigation.”
6 | P a g e
In Miller v. Minister of Pensions (1947) 2 All E.R. 372 Denning J, in the
Kings Bench held that:
“The evidence must reach the same degree of cogency as is required in
a criminal case before an accused person is found guilty. That degree is
well settled. It need not reach certainty, but it must carry high degree of
probability”
In the case of The King Vs. W. P. Buckley 43 NLR 474, it was stated by
Howard, C.J.:
“In arriving at a verdict of guilty, the majority of the jury must have viewed
the evidence in sections accepted and convicted the appellant on those
parts that were satisfactory and disregard those facts that pointed to the
improbability of the story put forward by the Crown. The jury should have
viewed the evidence as a whole. If they had done so, we are of opinion
that they must have had a reasonable doubt as to the guilt of the appellant.
The verdict is in our opinion, unreasonable, in as much as taken as a whole
the evidence does not support the conviction.”
In the case of Sivathasan v. Attorney General [2021] 2 SLR 290 it was held:
“A reasonable doubt is a real or actual and a substantial doubt, as
opposed to an imaginary or flimsy doubt that may arise in the mind of the
decider of facts (judge or the jury, as the case may be), following an
objective consideration of all the attendant facts and circumstances.
A reasonable doubt is not the type of doubt that arises due to incorrect,
abnormal or unreasonable comprehension of testimonies and other
material which amount to evidence presented at the trial, or due to
irrational fear, inappropriate sympathy, or unjustifiable mercy.”
7 | P a g e
PW1 had admitted that he had not entered the information received from his
private informant in the relevant books. PW1 in his evidence told court that
as an Officer-in-Charge he need not put that information in the relevant
books. Further, the PW1 had admitted that he had not mentioned in his
notes that he had knocked the door before the Appellant opened the door.
Section 1 of The Police Departmental Order No. A. 16, which lays down the
rules to be followed by the police in relation to note taking, is reproduced
below.
“For the purpose of investigating crime or accidents a notebook is the most
important record kept by a Police Officer. Notebooks should be regularly
examined, initialled and dated, by Officer-in-Charge of Stations, Districts,
Divisions and Provinces. The following rules regarding notebooks are to be
strictly observed.
(I) All entries in a notebook will be in ink or indelible pencil.
(II) Notes relating to inquiries will be headed with the date, time and
place at which they are written. On completion each note will be
initialled and dated by the Officer making the entry.
(III) No blank space will be left.
(IV) Nothing will be rubbed out, altered, or interpolated. Every error will
be crossed through by a single line so that it is still legible and will
be enclosed in brackets.
(V) Entries will be made in the Notebook on every matter arising in the
course of one’s official duties. Entries will also be made when an
Officer has by reason of sickness or other personal reasons to deviate
from orders given to him when he was sent out on duty.
(VI) No leaves will be removed from the Notebook.
(VII) Notebooks will be examined, initialled, and dated by the Reserve
Constable or Sergeant every time an Officer reports for or from duty.
At every Instruction Class Notebooks will be examined, initialled and
dated by the Officer holding such class.
8 | P a g e
(VIII) Officers when initialling and dating Notebooks will do so with a
different coloured ink or pencil when possible and in small characters
to save space.
(IX) Notebooks will always be carried when on duty, except on parade
unless specifically ordered to do so.
(X) All Police Officers will when leaving the Service return any unfinished
Notebook and re-fill to the Officer-in-Charge of the Station. Cost of
any re-fill not returned will be recovered and credited to revenue.”
As per the rules laid out in this Departmental Order, police officers are
obliged to immediately note down any information or intelligence regarding
criminal movements or attempted grave offences in their Personal Note Book.
If such information was received from a higher authority, the person’s details
must be noted and recorded in order to use such details as a basis for
subsequent arrests. All information regarding suspected offences must be
noted in writing. Such notes would be of utmost importance and would be
crucial in justifying police actions such as arrests and would also be required
for legal and transparent procedures.
PW1, in his evidence vehemently stated that he had not recorded the
information received in his Police Note Book. The relevant pages are re-
produced below:
Page 64 of the brief.

.shd'
Further, he had admitted that in his notes, he had not recorded that he
knocked on the door before the Appellant could open the door. The two
portions referred to above are very important as the Appellant had taken up
the position that he was not arrested as stated by the prosecution. According
to the Appellant, he was arrested at his sister’s house while sleeping. In this
instance, it is noted that, had PW1 taken down proper notes, he could have
correctly answered the questions fired at him during the cross examination.
Further, PW1 had not taken any endeavour to establish that the Appellant
and his family were the only occupants of the house from which the
Appellant was arrested.
PW1 had stated that he arrested the Appellant, as two warrants were pending
at that time.
Considering the evidence given by PW1, the probability of the raid as stated
by the prosecution raises a serious doubt which certainly affects the outcome
of the case.
In the same ground, the Learned President’s Counsel contended that the
Learned High Court Judge had allowed the prosecution to lead bad character
evidence under Section 54 of the Evidence Ordinance, thereby acting on that
evidence, the Learned High Court Judge had erroneously misdirected
himself.
10 | P a g e
Section 54 of the Evidence Ordinance states:
In criminal proceedings the fact that the accused person has a bad
character is irrelevant, unless evidence has been given that he has a good
character, in which case it becomes relevant.
Explanation 1.- This section does not apply to cases in which the bad
character of any person is itself a fact in issue.
Explanation 2.- A previous conviction is relevant as evidence of bad
character in such case.
As a result, the fact that the person charged is of good character is always
relevant in criminal proceedings. However, if the person has a bad character,
that fact is generally irrelevant. Hence, the prosecution should be mindful
not to lead character evidence of the accused, unless evidence has been given
that he has a good character. The trial judge also has a responsibility to
ensure that bad character evidence of the accused does not creep into the
court proceedings.
In R. G. Moses v. The Queen 75 NLR 121 the Court held:
“ that the conviction of the Appellant must be quashed on the ground
that the evidence of the previous conviction, which was inadmissible
according to Section 54 of the Evidence Ordinance, had been taken into
account in the trial judge’s judgment and was in a high degree
prejudicial to the Appellant. In such a case the substantial question is
whether or not the accused has been deprived a fair trial”.
In L.C. Fernando v. The Republic of Sri Lanka 79(II) NLR 313 the court
held (Wijesundera, J. dissenting) that:
“(a) that the evidence of ‘T’ was both irrelevant and inadmissible and in
view of the express prohibition against the admission of such evidence
11 | P a g e
in section 54 of the Evidence Ordinance and its highly prejudicial nature,
such evidence should have been excluded by the trial judge; the
improper reception of such evidence had resulted in the accused’s
chance of having a fair trial being prejudiced and in a failure of justice”.
In Makin v. Attorney General for New South Wales [1894] A.C 57 Lord
Herschell held that:
“It is not competent for the prosecution to adduce evidence tending to
show that the accused had been guilty of criminal acts other than those
covered by the indictment, for the purpose of leading to the conclusion
that he is likely, from his criminal conduct or character to have
committed the offence, for which he is being tried”.
As stated above, PW1 had stated that two warrants were pending against the
Appellant when he was arrested for this case.
Further, in the re-examination, the prosecution had led character evidence
as opposed to good character of the Appellant. The relevant portion is re-
produced below:
Page 76 of the brief.
m% ( uy;auhd óg fmr fuu pQos;j y`ÿkkjdo @
W ( fuu pQos; wmrdO j,g keUqre;djhla ;sfnk flfkla nj;a" úúO m%foaY j, kvq
;sfnk nj;a" ta iïnkaOfhka oekqula uf.;a ;snqKd'
The learned High Court had not taken any steps to disregard the evidence
which clearly discloses the character of the Appellant. The learned High
Court Judge without the said evidence had simply relied on the same when
he considered this case.
12 | P a g e
This evidence, with no doubt, clearly indicates that the prosecution had
placed bad character evidence, whereas, the Appellant had not presented
good character evidence. By leading this evidence, either accidently or
purposefully, the prosecution had paved the way for the character evidence
to creep into the proceedings. Ironically, the Learned High Court Judge had
considered this evidence in his judgment. Thus, the Appellant had been
denied a fair trial.
The Learned High Court Judge, before he could analyse the entire evidence
presented by both parties, by relying on the evidence given by PW1 and PW2,
concluded that the arrest and recovery of the firearm and ammunition from
the Appellant could be accepted beyond a reasonable doubt. This approach
of the Learned High Court Judge clearly demonstrates that he had been
greatly influenced by the bad character evidence which had creeped into the
court proceedings. The prosecution should not have led evidence which
contained bad character evidence of the Appellant. This leads to a denial of
a fair trial.
In the case of Shiv Kumar v. Hukam Chand and Anr [1999] 7 SCC 104 the
court observed that:
“The expected attitude of the Public Prosecutor while conducting
prosecution must be couched in fairness not only to the Court and to the
investigating agencies but to the accused as well. If an accused is
entitled to any legitimate benefit during trial the Public Prosecutor should
not scuttle/conceal it. On the contrary, it is the duty of the Public
Prosecutor to winch it to the force and make it available to the accused.
Even if the defence counsel overlooked it, Public Prosecutor has the
added responsibility to bring it to the notice of the Court if it comes to his
knowledge”
13 | P a g e
In the case of Medichetty Ramakistiah & ors. v. The State of Andhra
Pradesh (AIR 1959 A.P. 659) it was held that:
“A prosecution, to use a familiar phrase, ought not to be a persecution. The
principle that the Public Prosecutor should be scrupulously fair to the
licensed and present his case with detachment and without evincing any
anxiety to secure a conviction, is based upon high policy and as such courts
should be astute to suffer no inroad upon its integrity. Otherwise, there
will be no guarantee that the trial will be as fair to the accused as a
criminal trial ought to be. The State and the Public Prosecutor acting for it
are only supposed to be putting all the facts of the case before the Court to
obtain its decision thereon and not to obtain a conviction by any means
fair or foul.”
Hence, I conclude this ground of appeal has merit.
In the second ground of appeal, the learned President’s Counsel contended
that the learned High Court had reverted the presumption of innocence
towards the defence.
The Appellant in his dock statement admitted that he was arrested by officers
at his sister’s place. He vehemently denies being in possession of any
dangerous weapon in his custody. His position was that this case had been
fabricated against him.
When considering the defence evidence, the learned High Court Judge had
reverted the presumption of innocence stating that the Appellant has to
prove what he has taken in his defence. This is a clear misdirection as
reverting the presumption of innocence is unknown and not recognised in a
criminal prosecution.
In a criminal trial, as continuously stated by the Appellate Court the burden
of proving a case entirely rests on the hands of the prosecution and this
14 | P a g e
responsibility never shifts to the defence unless the defence takes up a plea
under a general or special exception of the Penal Code.
In H. M. Mahinda Herath v. The Attorney General CA/21/2003 in
Appellate Court Judgments (Unreported) 2005 at page 35-39 the court held
that:
“Where it was held that in a criminal case burden is always on the
prosecution to prove the charge levelled against the accused beyond
reasonable doubt. The trial judge must always bear in mind that the
accused is presumed to be innocent until the charge against the accused
is proved beyond reasonable grounds”.
The learned Counsel for the Appellant had cited two paragraphs of the
judgment and argued that the learned High Court Judge had reversed the
presumption of innocence and thereby denied a fair trial to the Appellant.
The learned High Court Judge in his judgment at Pages 152-153 stated as
follows:
3 and 4 of the brief.

15 | P a g e
The two paragraphs of the judgment cited above clearly demonstrates that
the learned High Court judge had reversed the burden of proof, which is
unknown to the criminal prosecution. As contended by the Counsel for the
Appellant this deviation had denied a fair trial to the Appellant.
The concept of fair trial is a fundamental principle in every judicial system.
In another sense, the notion of a fair trial secures justice. A trial in criminal
jurisprudence is a judicial examination or determination of the issues at the
hand of the Court to arrive at a conclusion whether the accused is guilty of
the offence or not. An accused has the right to a fair trial, where the
accused’s innocence or guilt in respect of the alleged crime will be
ascertained. This right remains an internationally recognised and respected
human right, as fair trials are essential in discovering the truth and would
therefore be of utmost importance to each party involved in a case.
The single most important criterion in evaluating the fairness of a trial is the
observance of the principle of equality of arms between the defence and the
prosecution. Equality of arms, which must be observed throughout the trial,
means that both parties are treated in a manner which ensures their
procedurally equal position during the course of a trial.
In this case as stated earlier, the Learned Trial Judge had come to a
conclusion that the evidence given by PW1 and PW2 could be accepted as
true regarding the raid conducted in respect of the Appellant. This clearly
shows that the Learned High Court Judge has been highly influenced by the
inadmissible or prejudicial evidence led by the prosecution. Further, the
Learned High Court Judge has not mentioned in his judgment that he
disregards the inadmissible or prejudicial evidence in deciding this case.
Hence, this ground also has merit.
The pleaded grounds of appeal above have merits which certainly disturb the
judgment of the learned High Court Judge.
16 | P a g e
Due to the aforesaid reasons, I set aside the conviction and the sentence
dated 23/03/2023 imposed on the Appellant by the learned High Court
Judge of Anuradhapura. Therefore, I acquit him from both charges.
Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
The Registrar of this Court is directed to send this judgment to the High
Court of Anuradhapura along with the original case record.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
